question: 2. Let \( R \) be the set of all real numbers and \( S \) the set of all subsets of the positive integers. Construct a function \( f \) whose domain is \( R \) and whose range is in \( S \), such that \( f(a) \) is a proper subset of \( f(b) \) whenever \( a<b \).
solution:
Solution. Let \( \phi \) be an enumeration of the rationals, i.e., a bijective map from \( \mathbf{N} \) to \( \mathbf{Q} \), where \( \mathbf{N} \) is the set of positive integers and \( \mathbf{Q} \) is the set of rational numbers.

Define a function \( f \) as follows
\[
f(r)=\{n: \phi(n)<r\}
\]
where \( r \) is a real number. Then the domain of \( f \) is \( \mathbf{R} \) and the values of \( f \) are subsets of \( \mathbf{N} \).

If \( a<b \) and \( n \in f(a) \). then \( \phi(n)<a<b \), so \( n \in f(b) \). Thus \( f(a) \subseteq \) \( f(b) \). Moreover. the inclusion is proper because there is a rational number between \( a \) and \( b \) and therefore an integer \( p \) such that \( a<\phi(p)<b \). so \( p \in f(b) \) but \( p \notin f^{\prime}(a) \).

Remark. For the proof it is sufficient that \( \phi: \mathbf{N} \rightarrow \mathbf{R} \) have dense range.

descriptive_long.question: 2. Let \( realset \) be the set of all real numbers and \( subsetsp \) the set of all subsets of the positive integers. Construct a function \( funcset \) whose domain is \( realset \) and whose range is in \( subsetsp \), such that \( funcset(lowernum) \) is a proper subset of \( funcset(highernum) \) whenever \( lowernum<highernum \).
descriptive_long.solution:
Let \( rationum \) be an enumeration of the rationals, i.e., a bijective map from \( \mathbf{natset} \) to \( \mathbf{ratset} \), where \( \mathbf{natset} \) is the set of positive integers and \( \mathbf{ratset} \) is the set of rational numbers.

Define a function \( funcset \) as follows
\[
funcset(realnum)=\{indexnat: rationum(indexnat)<realnum\}
\]
where \( realnum \) is a real number. Then the domain of \( funcset \) is \( \mathbf{realset} \) and the values of \( funcset \) are subsets of \( \mathbf{natset} \).

If \( lowernum<highernum \) and \( indexnat \in funcset(lowernum) \), then \( rationum(indexnat)<lowernum<highernum \), so \( indexnat \in funcset(highernum) \). Thus \( funcset(lowernum) \subseteq funcset(highernum) \). Moreover, the inclusion is proper because there is a rational number between \( lowernum \) and \( highernum \) and therefore an integer \( midpoint \) such that \( lowernum<rationum(midpoint)<highernum \), so \( midpoint \in funcset(highernum) \) but \( midpoint \notin funcset^{\prime}(lowernum) \).

Remark. For the proof it is sufficient that \( rationum: \mathbf{natset} \rightarrow \mathbf{realset} \) have dense range.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 2. Let \( orangutan \) be the set of all real numbers and \( firebrand \) the set of all subsets of the positive integers. Construct a function \( lamplight \) whose domain is \( orangutan \) and whose range is in \( firebrand \), such that \( lamplight(zoetrope) \) is a proper subset of \( lamplight(tapestry) \) whenever \( zoetrope<tapestry \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Let \( whirligig \) be an enumeration of the rationals, i.e., a bijective map from \( \mathbf{salamander} \) to \( \mathbf{buttercup} \), where \( \mathbf{salamander} \) is the set of positive integers and \( \mathbf{buttercup} \) is the set of rational numbers.

Define a function \( lamplight \) as follows
\[
lamplight(lullabies)=\{pendulum: whirligig(pendulum)<lullabies\}
\]
where \( lullabies \) is a real number. Then the domain of \( lamplight \) is \( \mathbf{orangutan} \) and the values of \( lamplight \) are subsets of \( \mathbf{salamander} \).

If \( zoetrope<tapestry \) and \( pendulum \in lamplight(zoetrope) \), then \( whirligig(pendulum)<zoetrope<tapestry \), so \( pendulum \in lamplight(tapestry) \). Thus \( lamplight(zoetrope) \subseteq \lamplight(tapestry) \). Moreover, the inclusion is proper because there is a rational number between \( zoetrope \) and \( tapestry \) and therefore an integer \( benediction \) such that \( zoetrope<whirligig(benediction)<tapestry \), so \( benediction \in lamplight(tapestry) \) but \( benediction \notin lamplight^{\prime}(zoetrope) \).

Remark. For the proof it is sufficient that \( whirligig: \mathbf{salamander} \rightarrow \mathbf{orangutan} \) have dense range.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 2. Let \( imaginaryset \) be the set of all real numbers and \( singularset \) the set of all subsets of the positive integers. Construct a function \( antifunction \) whose domain is \( imaginaryset \) and whose range is in \( singularset \), such that \( antifunction(zenithless) \) is a proper subset of \( antifunction(nadirplus) \) whenever \( zenithless<nadirplus \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Let \( chaosmap \) be an enumeration of the rationals, i.e., a bijective map from \( \mathbf{negativereals} \) to \( \mathbf{irrational} \), where \( \mathbf{negativereals} \) is the set of positive integers and \( \mathbf{irrational} \) is the set of rational numbers.

Define a function \( antifunction \) as follows
\[
antifunction(unrealvalue)=\{fractional: chaosmap(fractional)<unrealvalue\}
\]
where \( unrealvalue \) is a real number. Then the domain of \( antifunction \) is \( \mathbf{imaginaryset} \) and the values of \( antifunction \) are subsets of \( \mathbf{negativereals} \).

If \( zenithless<nadirplus \) and \( fractional \in antifunction(zenithless) \). then \( chaosmap(fractional)<zenithless<nadirplus \), so \( fractional \in antifunction(nadirplus) \). Thus \( antifunction(zenithless) \subseteq \) \( antifunction(nadirplus) \). Moreover. the inclusion is proper because there is a rational number between \( zenithless \) and \( nadirplus \) and therefore an integer \( continuous \) such that \( zenithless<chaosmap(continuous)<nadirplus \). so \( continuous \in antifunction(nadirplus) \) but \( continuous \notin antifunction^{\prime}(zenithless) \).

Remark. For the proof it is sufficient that \( chaosmap: \mathbf{negativereals} \rightarrow \mathbf{imaginaryset} \) have dense range.

garbled_string.question: 2. Let \( xvhcujak \) be the set of all real numbers and \( rqepgnti \) the set of all subsets of the positive integers. Construct a function \( ujrmbkaw \) whose domain is \( xvhcujak \) and whose range is in \( rqepgnti \), such that \( ujrmbkaw(quxjfpst) \) is a proper subset of \( ujrmbkaw(yzmrhdlk) \) whenever \( quxjfpst<yzmrhdlk \).
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Let \( wvkhytsq \) be an enumeration of the rationals, i.e., a bijective map from \( \mathbf{tygrldwe} \) to \( \mathbf{hozsjvkm} \), where \( \mathbf{tygrldwe} \) is the set of positive integers and \( \mathbf{hozsjvkm} \) is the set of rational numbers.

Define a function \( ujrmbkaw \) as follows
\[
ujrmbkaw(vnpqzscr)=\{ktwhgava: wvkhytsq(ktwhgava)<vnpqzscr\}
\]
where \( vnpqzscr \) is a real number. Then the domain of \( ujrmbkaw \) is \( \mathbf{xvhcujak} \) and the values of \( ujrmbkaw \) are subsets of \( \mathbf{tygrldwe} \).

If \( quxjfpst<yzmrhdlk \) and \( ktwhgava \in ujrmbkaw(quxjfpst) \), then \( wvkhytsq(ktwhgava)<quxjfpst<yzmrhdlk \), so \( ktwhgava \in ujrmbkaw(yzmrhdlk) \). Thus \( ujrmbkaw(quxjfpst) \subseteq ujrmbkaw(yzmrhdlk) \). Moreover, the inclusion is proper because there is a rational number between \( quxjfpst \) and \( yzmrhdlk \) and therefore an integer \( lomdnceq \) such that \( quxjfpst<wvkhytsq(lomdnceq)<yzmrhdlk \), so \( lomdnceq \in ujrmbkaw(yzmrhdlk) \) but \( lomdnceq \notin ujrmbkaw^{\prime}(quxjfpst) \).

Remark. For the proof it is sufficient that \( wvkhytsq: \mathbf{tygrldwe} \rightarrow \mathbf{xvhcujak} \) have dense range.

kernel_variant.question:
Let \(P=\{p_{1}<p_{2}<p_{3}<\dots\}\) be the sequence of prime numbers in their natural order and let \(\mathcal P(P)\) denote its power set.  
For a subset \(A\subseteq P\) put  

\[
d(A)=\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{|A\cap\{p_{1},\dots ,p_{n}\}|}{n},
\]

whenever the limit exists; this is called the index-density of \(A\) in the primes.  

Define the logistic map  

\[
\sigma:\mathbb R\longrightarrow(0,1),\qquad 
\sigma(t)=\frac{1}{1+e^{-t}} .
\]

Construct an explicit function  

\[
F:\mathbb R\longrightarrow
\bigl\{A\subseteq P:\,d(A)\text{ exists}\bigr\}
\]

satisfying simultaneously  

1. (Order-monotonicity) \(a<b\;\Longrightarrow\;F(a)\subsetneq F(b)\);  

2. (Prescribed density) \(d\bigl(F(a)\bigr)=\sigma(a)\quad\forall a\in\mathbb R\);  

3. (Left-continuity with respect to inclusion)  
  If \(a_{k}\uparrow a\) then \(F(a)=\bigcup_{k=1}^{\infty}F(a_{k})\).
kernel_variant.solution:
The failure of the previous block-by-block construction was the appearance of large local oscillations of the ratio  

\[
\frac{|F(a)\cap\{p_{1},\dots ,p_{n}\}|}{n}
\]

inside every block.  To remove this defect we discard the ``block'' idea altogether and base the selection on an equidistributed sequence of real numbers.

Step 0 - A universal sequence of thresholds  
Fix the irrational number \(\alpha=\sqrt2\).  
For every integer \(k\ge1\) set  

\[
\theta_{k}= \bigl\{k\alpha\bigr\}\in(0,1),
\]

where \(\{\cdot\}\) denotes the fractional part.  
By Weyl's equidistribution theorem the sequence \((\theta_k)_{k\ge1}\) is uniformly distributed in the unit interval, i.e.

\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{1}{n}\bigl|\{1\le k\le n:\theta_k<x\}\bigr|=x
\qquad\forall x\in[0,1].
\tag{1}
\]

Step 1 - Definition of \(F(a)\)  
For each real \(a\) define  

\[
F(a)=\bigl\{p_k:\theta_k<\sigma(a)\bigr\}.
\tag{2}
\]

The construction is completely explicit: the only ingredients are the
standard enumeration of the primes, the irrational \(\sqrt2\), and
elementary arithmetic.

Step 2 - Order-monotonicity  
Let \(a<b\).  
Because \(\sigma\) is strictly increasing, \(\sigma(a)<\sigma(b)\).  
If \(\theta_k<\sigma(a)\), then automatically \(\theta_k<\sigma(b)\); hence  
\(F(a)\subseteq F(b)\).  
The inclusion is proper because (1) guarantees the existence of infinitely many indices \(k\) with  

\[
\sigma(a)<\theta_k<\sigma(b),
\]

and each such index contributes a prime contained in \(F(b)\setminus F(a)\).

Step 3 - Computation of the density  
Put \(x=\sigma(a)\in(0,1)\).  
For every \(n\ge1\), let  

\[
S_n(a):=|F(a)\cap\{p_{1},\dots ,p_{n}\}|
       =\bigl|\{1\le k\le n:\theta_k<x\}\bigr|.
\]

Divide by \(n\) and pass to the limit.  By (1) we obtain  

\[
d\bigl(F(a)\bigr)=\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{S_n(a)}{n}=x=\sigma(a),
\]

establishing property (2).

Step 4 - Left-continuity  
Assume \(a_1<a_2<\dots\uparrow a\).  
Write \(x_k=\sigma(a_k)\) and \(x=\sigma(a)\); then \(x_k\uparrow x\).  

Fix an index \(j\ge1\).  
* If \(\theta_j<x\), choose \(\varepsilon>0\) with \(\theta_j<x-\varepsilon\).
Since \(x_k\to x\), we have \(x_k>x-\tfrac{\varepsilon}{2}\) for all
large \(k\), hence \(\theta_j<x_k\) eventually and \(p_j\in F(a_k)\)
for all sufficiently large \(k\).  
* If \(\theta_j\ge x\), then \(\theta_j<x_k\) never occurs, so
\(p_j\notin\bigcup_{k}F(a_k)\).

Consequently

\[
\bigcup_{k=1}^{\infty}F(a_k)=F(a),
\]

i.e. property (3) holds.

All three requested properties are satisfied; therefore the explicit map (2) is the required solution.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Additional quantitative constraint.  Unlike the original tasks, the new problem prescribes the exact index–density \(d(F(a))\) for every \(a\), forcing the constructor simultaneously to control inclusion and an asymptotic ratio.

• Non-trivial asymptotics.  Verifying the densities demands an error estimate that decays exponentially fast, calling upon careful block-size selection and limit calculations absent from the earlier variants.

• Continuity requirement.  Property (3) obliges the competitor to establish a set-theoretic analogue of left-continuity, adding another logical layer to the argument.

• Ingenious block partition.  Choosing block lengths \(2^{n}\) is crucial: shorter blocks would not drive the error term to zero, longer blocks would complicate monotonicity.  The solver must invent and justify this geometry.

• Combining several ideas.  One needs (i) monotone integer approximations of the logistic map, (ii) a tailor-made prime partition, (iii) density estimates, and (iv) a limit argument—all interacting at once.

These ingredients require deeper insight and longer reasoning chains than the enumeration-based solution of the original and current kernel variants, making the enhanced version significantly more challenging.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let \(P=\{p_{1}<p_{2}<p_{3}<\dots\}\) be the sequence of prime numbers in their natural order and let \(\mathcal P(P)\) denote its power set.  
For a subset \(A\subseteq P\) put

\]

\[
\sigma:\mathbb R\longrightarrow(0,1),\qquad 
\sigma(t)=\frac{1}{1+e^{-t}} .
\]

Construct an explicit function  

\[
F:\mathbb R\longrightarrow
\bigl\{A\subseteq P:\,d(A)\text{ exists}\bigr\}
\]

1. (Order-monotonicity) \(a<b\;\Longrightarrow\;F(a)\subsetneq F(b)\);  

2. (Prescribed density) \(d\bigl(F(a)\bigr)=\sigma(a)\quad\forall a\in\mathbb R\);  

3. (Left-continuity with respect to inclusion)  
  If \(a_{k}\uparrow a\) then \(F(a)=\bigcup_{k=1}^{\infty}F(a_{k})\).
original_kernel_variant.solution:
The failure of the previous block-by-block construction was the appearance of large local oscillations of the ratio  

\[

inside every block.  To remove this defect we discard the ``block'' idea altogether and base the selection on an equidistributed sequence of real numbers.

Step 0 - A universal sequence of thresholds  
Fix the irrational number \(\alpha=\sqrt2\).

\]

where \(\{\cdot\}\) denotes the fractional part.

\[

\]

and each such index contributes a prime contained in \(F(b)\setminus F(a)\).

Step 3 - Computation of the density  
Put \(x=\sigma(a)\in(0,1)\).

\[

\]

\]

Step 4 - Left-continuity  
Assume \(a_1<a_2<\dots\uparrow a\).  
Write \(x_k=\sigma(a_k)\) and \(x=\sigma(a)\); then \(x_k\uparrow x\).

Consequently

\[

\]

All three requested properties are satisfied; therefore the explicit map (2) is the required solution.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Additional quantitative constraint.  Unlike the original tasks, the new problem prescribes the exact index–density \(d(F(a))\) for every \(a\), forcing the constructor simultaneously to control inclusion and an asymptotic ratio.

• Non-trivial asymptotics.  Verifying the densities demands an error estimate that decays exponentially fast, calling upon careful block-size selection and limit calculations absent from the earlier variants.

• Continuity requirement.  Property (3) obliges the competitor to establish a set-theoretic analogue of left-continuity, adding another logical layer to the argument.

• Ingenious block partition.  Choosing block lengths \(2^{n}\) is crucial: shorter blocks would not drive the error term to zero, longer blocks would complicate monotonicity.  The solver must invent and justify this geometry.

• Combining several ideas.  One needs (i) monotone integer approximations of the logistic map, (ii) a tailor-made prime partition, (iii) density estimates, and (iv) a limit argument—all interacting at once.

These ingredients require deeper insight and longer reasoning chains than the enumeration-based solution of the original and current kernel variants, making the enhanced version significantly more challenging.
